one_line_takeaway: SWaRL trains a code LLM via GRPO reinforcement learning with a hybrid execution-correctness + co-trained-detector reward (LoRA-adapted) to embed IP watermarks directly into generated code's behavior rather than via inference-time token biasing or handcrafted rules, achieving Pass@1 that meets or exceeds the non-watermarked baseline while cutting refactoring-attack AUROC degradation to roughly 2-9% versus 11-17% for token-level statistical watermarking baselines (WLLM, SWEET).
key_finding: On the main benchmark table (Table 1, HumanEval/MBPP/HumanEval+/MBPP+), SWaRL achieves the best Pass@1 among watermarking methods on every benchmark and even exceeds the non-watermarked Base+SFT baseline on some: e.g., HumanEval Pass@1 55.23 (Base+SFT, no watermark) vs 58.22 (SWaRL), Pass@10 69.28 vs 78.64. Prior watermark baselines trade off correctness for detectability: on HumanEval, WLLM raises AUROC to 0.771 but drops Pass@1 to 30.82; SWEET reaches AUROC 0.798 with Pass@1 42.01; EXP-edit preserves Pass@1 (55.00) but AUROC is near-random (0.486 on HumanEval, 0.508 on HumanEval+). SWaRL's AUROC values are 0.725/0.908/0.705/0.772 on HumanEval/MBPP/HumanEval+/MBPP+ respectively -- generally lower than WLLM/SWEET's peak AUROC on some benchmarks but paired with much higher Pass@1/Pass@10, i.e. a stated 'better balance' rather than a strict dominance on AUROC alone. Under refactoring-attack robustness (Table 2), SWaRL shows the smallest AUROC degradation: 3.45% (QW Attack, HumanEval) and 9.40% (QW Attack, MBPP), versus WLLM's 14.14%/17.08% and SWEET's 11.78%/12.36%; under DS Attack, SWaRL degrades 5.66%/2.21% versus WLLM 14.01%/14.04% and SWEET 11.41%/16.82%. SWaRL also retains the highest post-attack AUROC on MBPP under both attacks (0.819 and 0.884). The paper's abstract-level claim of '6.4% average AUROC degradation' matches this robustness pattern. Under variable-renaming attacks (Figure 4, MBPP, 10/30/50% identifier perturbation), SWaRL maintains AUROC largely stable while WLLM and SWEET degrade as renaming ratio increases. Latency (Table 3): SWaRL generation cost is 0.039 s/token (vs WLLM 0.040, SWEET 0.044, EXP-edit 0.023) and SWaRL detection cost is the lowest at 0.0002 s/token, versus WLLM 0.001, SWEET 0.004, and EXP-edit 0.588 s/token.
core_contribution: SWaRL is a reinforcement-learning-based code watermarking framework for code LLMs. It fine-tunes an actor code LLM with GRPO (Group Relative Policy Optimization) using a hybrid reward that combines (1) a watermark-detection score from a jointly co-trained, frozen-then-periodically-updated detector model (microsoft/codebert-base) that estimates whether a code sample was generated by the watermarked actor, (2) an execution-based correctness score computed by running generated code against unit tests, and (3) a KL-divergence penalty to the reference policy for training stability. The actor is fine-tuned via LoRA adapters injected into attention/MLP layers rather than full-parameter updates, keeping the watermarking behavior modular and lightweight. The detector is periodically re-trained (co-training) on a mix of recent actor-generated samples (positive) and reference/external code (negative) via binary cross-entropy, so it tracks the evolving actor policy over training. This replaces prior approaches that either (a) bias token-generation probabilities at inference time with green/red token lists (inference-based watermarking, e.g., WLLM, SWEET), which can break code syntax/functionality, or (b) apply handcrafted syntactic transformation rules (rule-based/neural-based watermarking), which are vulnerable to refactoring attacks that restructure the code.
threat_model: The paper's own threat model (Section 3, 'Threat model'): the code LLM owner hosts the model as a cloud API; users receive only the final watermarked code and have no access to the generation/watermarking process. An adversarial user has full access to the watermarked code (can read, edit, refactor, execute it) but does not have access to the provider's watermarking key or the insertion/verification mechanisms. The attacker's goal is to deactivate watermark verification (prevent correct message/detector recovery) via post-processing/refactoring/adversarial transformation while preserving functional correctness of the code (since the adversary still needs the code to work). This is an IP-protection / code-provenance / authorship-attribution threat model, not a jailbreak or prompt-injection threat model.
limitations: The paper's own stated limitations (Section 5): SWaRL currently operates at the source-code, function level; extending to compiled/binary-level code watermarking (for settings where source is unavailable) is left to future work, as is repository-level watermarking, which the authors note introduces a substantially larger and more structurally diverse adversarial modification space than function-level code. Additional limitations evident from the results but not explicitly flagged by the authors: SWaRL's AUROC is not uniformly the best among all methods pre-attack (e.g., WLLM/SWEET post higher AUROC than SWaRL on HumanEval and HumanEval+ before attack -- 0.771/0.798 vs SWaRL's 0.725, and 0.799/0.765 vs SWaRL's 0.705), so the claimed advantage is a joint correctness+robustness tradeoff rather than best-in-class detectability alone. The detector is a relatively small BERT-scale model (codebert-base, 512-token context) trained on only 10,000 samples per stage, and robustness evaluation uses only two specific attacker LLMs (Qwen2.5-3B-Instruct, DeepSeek-Coder-7B-Instruct-v1.5) with a single refactoring prompt strategy, which may not represent the full space of possible adversarial rewriting/obfuscation strategies.

datasets_used: OpenCoder-LLM dataset (SFT, detector pretraining, and GRPO co-training splits)
quantitative_results: HumanEval Pass@1: Base+SFT 55.23, EXP-edit 55.00, WLLM 30.82, SWEET 42.01, SWaRL 58.22
HumanEval Pass@10: Base+SFT 69.28, EXP-edit 69.09, WLLM 51.07, SWEET 58.49, SWaRL 78.64
HumanEval AUROC: EXP-edit 0.486, WLLM 0.771, SWEET 0.798, SWaRL 0.725
MBPP Pass@1: Base+SFT 52.52, EXP-edit 52.05, WLLM 25.54, SWEET 35.49, SWaRL 53.40
MBPP AUROC: EXP-edit 0.598, WLLM 0.890, SWEET 0.898, SWaRL 0.908
HumanEval+ Pass@1: Base+SFT 48.81, EXP-edit 48.63, WLLM 27.24, SWEET 37.82, SWaRL 52.36; AUROC: EXP-edit 0.508, WLLM 0.799, SWEET 0.765, SWaRL 0.705
MBPP+ Pass@1: Base+SFT 65.26, EXP-edit 65.38, WLLM 31.93, SWEET 42.79, SWaRL 66.20; AUROC: EXP-edit 0.575, WLLM 0.825, SWEET 0.841, SWaRL 0.772
Refactoring attack (Table 2) AUROC degradation, QW Attack: EXP-edit 2.26%/7.02% (HumanEval/MBPP), WLLM 14.14%/17.08%, SWEET 11.78%/12.36%, SWaRL 3.45%/9.40%
Refactoring attack (Table 2) AUROC degradation, DS Attack: EXP-edit 5.35%/5.85%, WLLM 14.01%/14.04%, SWEET 11.41%/16.82%, SWaRL 5.66%/2.21%
SWaRL post-attack AUROC on MBPP: 0.819 (QW Attack) and 0.884 (DS Attack), the highest among methods under both attacks
Latency (Table 3, seconds/token): generation -- EXP-edit 0.023, WLLM 0.040, SWEET 0.044, SWaRL 0.039; detection -- EXP-edit 0.588, WLLM 0.001, SWEET 0.004, SWaRL 0.0002
Abstract-level summary claim: SWaRL exhibits 6.4% average AUROC degradation across evaluated datasets under refactoring attacks
baselines_compared: EXP-edit (Kuditipudi et al., robust distortion-free LM watermarking, adapted as code baseline)
WLLM (Kirchenbauer et al. green/red-list inference-time LLM watermarking, delta=3, gamma=0.25)
SWEET (Lee et al., 'Who wrote this code?' code watermarking via green/red token lists, delta=3, gamma=0.25)
